full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.60|> - St. Luke knows how to set a scene, doesn't he?<|3.48|> <|4.76|> When Jesus had been baptized, heaven opened,<|7.68|> <|7.68|> and the Holy Spirit descended upon him<|11.10|> <|11.10|> in bodily form like a dove.<|13.22|> <|14.20|> He places you right in the middle of the River Jordan<|18.40|> <|18.40|> with Jesus.<|19.76|> <|20.80|> You can feel the water rushing about you.<|24.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> and the fish nibbling at your legs.<|3.42|> <|4.18|> You can taste the salty river water in the air.<|7.24|> <|8.70|> You can almost, if you squint hard enough,<|11.14|> <|11.90|> see the Holy Spirit descending.<|13.96|> <|15.66|> In the words of this English translation,<|18.48|> <|19.12|> like a dove.<|19.96|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Christians love that dove. Doves on Bibles, doves on vestments, doves on baptismal gowns.<|12.00|> <|12.00|> Christians love doves.<|17.00|> <|17.00|> But in the original language, the word here can be translated "pigeon."<|25.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Yes, pigeon.<|5.40|> <|5.40|> And the Holy Spirit descended upon him in bodily form like a pigeon.<|13.98|> <|13.98|> This minor detail makes Jesus' baptism a little more relatable.<|19.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> because when you walk onto 14th Avenue after this service,<|5.00|> <|5.08|> you're more likely to see a pigeon than a dove.<|8.06|> <|9.88|> St. Luke's account is not the sanitized stained glass version<|14.88|> <|15.44|> that includes a serene river bank,<|18.34|> <|18.34|> a white gentle dove, and a tiny portion of water.<|22.62|> <|22.62|> No, St. Luke's account of this story includes a muddy river,<|28.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> A crowd of sinners and a bird so common it makes us chuckle.<|6.24|> <|7.70|> And yet amidst the humor and humility of this vignette from Jesus' life, something beautiful and remarkable happens.<|16.56|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Peace.<|1.14|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Mark Oakley, a priest and recent guest at the Dean's Forum, says the following about Jesus' baptism:<|9.50|> <|9.50|> "Jesus gets pushed under the surface. You can only hear your own heart beating.<|18.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And all the noise from the shore, the opinions, the dogmas, the criticisms, and empty chatter all get drowned out.<|10.00|> <|10.00|> It's just him for a second or two, and then pulled out, he takes a deep breath of fresh air.<|19.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> we are told that he then hears a voice,<|3.14|> <|3.74|> not from the shore, but from heaven.<|7.00|> <|8.10|> This is the one voice that matters,<|10.18|> <|10.18|> and it tells him, you are mine, I love you,<|13.84|> <|14.16|> you make me happy, end quote.<|16.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It is a compelling story for the reasons Mark gives.<|5.28|> <|5.28|> It is only when we suspend the cacophonies of the shore and our plunge beneath life's<|12.48|> <|12.48|> many surfaces that we can hear the one voice that matters.<|18.40|> Jesus, the voice of God is living and audible.<|17.54|> <|18.62|> But he had to abandon the noise of the shore to hear it.<|22.52|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank split second after a wreck,<|3.00|> <|3.00|> the bedside of a dying loved one,<|7.00|> <|7.00|> the stillness of a waiting room<|10.00|> <|10.00|> just before the doctor walks in to say whether it's cancer<|14.00|> <|14.00|> or if she made it or if you even have time to say goodbye.<|18.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Or the fog when the anesthesia is wearing off and it's 3:00 AM and it's just you and God and your breath.<|10.00|> <|10.00|> Your beating heart.<|13.00|> <|13.00|> Ba-dum.<|15.00|> <|15.00|> Ba-dum.<|17.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Baptism, like the rest of the sacraments, does something words alone cannot do.<|7.00|> <|7.00|> For instance, I can stand here every Sunday and say, "God loves you. God loves you. You are forgiven. God loves you. God loves you."<|16.00|> <|16.00|> For some people, that's all they need to hear. It works for them.<|22.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> For others, and I assume this is probably the majority of us,<|5.00|> <|5.00|> the tangible, incarnate, concrete experience of water,<|12.00|> <|12.00|> the sound of it as it rushes into the font and over your head,<|16.00|> <|16.00|> the warmth of it on your skin, the sight of it at every entrance of this sacred space.<|23.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> is the outward and visible sign needed to remember that you and I are in fact loved beyond measure and are held in the loving arms of God.<|11.86|> <|13.00|> To paraphrase a very wise bishop, baptism makes real.<|18.20|> <|19.28|> Baptism does not make true.<|21.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This makes the sacrament of the altar,<|3.86|> <|4.68|> the celebration of Holy Communion,<|6.84|> <|7.18|> the body and blood of Christ as bread and wine,<|10.02|> <|10.82|> so powerful.<|11.84|> <|13.30|> Some theologians are fond of saying that Holy Communion<|16.04|> <|16.04|> is the repeatable dimension of Holy Baptism.<|19.52|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> As we place our palms before us and hear those mysterious words, the body of Christ, and instinctively grip the manna from heaven, something extraordinary is happening.<|15.74|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.44|> Without prompting, we place the sacramental bread<|5.38|> <|5.38|> on our tongues and swallow the incarnate God.<|9.96|> <|11.44|> It is in that moment of silent intimacy<|14.98|> <|15.90|> that an organic amen.<|18.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> which is Hebrew for yes, emerges from our souls.<|5.00|> <|6.90|> Because that's all that can be said when we hear,<|10.00|> <|10.00|> "You are mine, I love you, I delight in you."<|14.44|> <|15.48|> This unutterable physical experience<|18.98|> <|18.98|> evades the rhetorical cul-de-sacs<|22.14|> <|22.14|> of bumper sticker theology,<|24.12|> <|24.12|> religious sound bites, and lazy platitudes.<|27.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And it draws us back to this community, to this place, when wildfires encroach on our suburbs, viruses betray our bodies, and uncertainty rattles our souls.<|15.80|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So to every child and adult who will wade in the water with Jesus today,<|7.60|> <|8.70|> the pigeon is here, and it is waiting for you.<|14.58|> <|15.80|> Amen.<|16.38|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Amen.<|1.00|><|endoftext|>
